behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real git rename detection plus CLI parsing/aggregation via subprocess output. A mock diff can miss real `git` rename heuristics or output-format edge cases, so mocked tests can pass while the real CLI misclassifies renames.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real temp git repo, commit a file, rename it, run the CLI as a subprocess, and assert the report increments `renamed` only (not `added`+`deleted`) for that change.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Binary diff handling failures often arise from real subprocess/stdout encoding and real git binary-diff behavior. In-process fakes usually return sanitized text and can hide decode/crash bugs.
behaviors.test_description: In a real git repo, add/modify a binary file (e.g., random bytes), run the CLI subprocess, assert exit success and that binary change is counted in the expected category.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: This depends on real git behavior for unborn HEAD / no-commit repos (often special exit codes/messages). Mocking git responses can hide failure paths caused by actual subprocess return codes and stderr.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a new git repo with zero commits, run the CLI subprocess, assert non-error exit and an empty structured report.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end correctness claim against real repository history; the key risk is mismatch between real git history semantics and tool aggregation. Fakes can mirror implementation assumptions and miss real-history discrepancies.
behaviors.test_description: Build a real repo history with known adds/deletes/renames/modifies across commits, run the CLI subprocess, and compare reported counts against independently computed expected counts from git history ground truth.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Behavior depends on real git index vs working-tree semantics and CLI interaction with git commands. Mocking hides real staging-area differences and command-flag behavior.
behaviors.test_description: In a real repo, create equivalent file changes in two setups (staged-only vs unstaged-only), run the CLI subprocess in each setup, and assert byte-for-byte identical structured output.
